message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The number of published clinical practice guidelines and recommendations related to SARS-CoV-2 infections causing COVID-19 has rapidly increased. However, insufficient consideration of appropriate methodologies in the guideline development could lead to misleading information, uncertainty among professionals, and potentially harmful actions for patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Purpose</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Rapid systematic review of clinical practice guidelines and recommendations in the context of COVID-19 to explore if basic methodological standards of guideline development have been met.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    MEDLINE [PubMed], CINAHL [Ebsco], Trip and manual search; from Feb 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    2020 until April 27
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    2020.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study selection</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>All types of healthcare workers providing any kind of healthcare to any patient population in any setting.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data extraction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    At least two reviewers independently extracted guideline characteristics, conducted critical appraisal according to
                    <jats:italic>The Appraisal of Guidelines for Research and Evaluation Instrument</jats:italic>
                    (AGREE II) and classified the guidelines using the
                    <jats:italic>Association of the Scientific Medical Societies (AWMF) Guidance Manual and Rules for Guideline Development</jats:italic>
                    . We plan six-month updates (living review).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data synthesis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    There were 1342 titles screened and 188 guidelines included. The highest average AGREE II domain score was 89% for
                    <jats:italic>scope and purpose</jats:italic>
                    , the lowest for
                    <jats:italic>rigor of development</jats:italic>
                    (25%). Only eight guidelines (4%) were based on a systematic literature search and a structured consensus process by representative experts (classified as the highest methodological quality, S3 according to AWMF). Patients were only included in the development of one guideline. A process for regular updates was described in 27 guidelines (14%).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Limitations</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Methodological focus only.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Despite clear scope, most publications fell short of basic methodological standards of guideline development. Future research should monitor the evolving methodological quality of the guidelines and their updates over time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Registration/Publication</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The protocol was published at
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://www.researchgate.net">www.researchgate.net</jats:ext-link>
                    , DOI: 10.13140/RG.2.2.21293.51689. Preliminary results are publicly available on medRxiv.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Clinical practice guidelines and recommendations in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic: systematic review and critical appraisal
